Today's training is on the probe. Guys, the probe is vital in the success of the cell. It's when we're digging for goals, digging for pain, and that's really why people buy. In order to get to where you can do a good probe, you have to do a good introduction and you have to do a good blast. If you don't do it in the process, then it makes it tough to get a good probe. So there's a rule in cells, and I don't know if any of you have listened to Tony Robbins, Garrett calls him Uncle Tony, but there's an 80-20 rule in cells and it stands for a couple different things. First of all, 20% of the people in cells make 80% of the money. It's just how it is. But the good side of it is it's a learned skill and you're constantly reading books and learning and listening to other people to get to that point. But the biggest part of what we do with the 80-20 rule is that your client should, you should be listening 80% of the time, especially in the probe. In what I say speaking 20% of the time, you've all heard us say this is question-based selling. Okay, especially in the probe. You should get through your entire probe without making a single comment. It should all be questions. See, when you're going through the blast, the introduction is obviously some questions. The blast, you should get through the entire blast. I think the script has one question in it. Randy and I knew that you should get through a blast without asking a single question, without taking a pause, just hammer upon the blast, get him excited. But then when you get into the probe, you take a step back and all you're doing is taking notes and asking questions. So on your script, what's the first question in the probe? What type of internet connection do you have? High speed or dial it? Why do you think that is? Why is that the first connect or first question? Just to see if they're going to be able to do the online training. Okay, every single person that we've talked to has bought something from us already online. Okay, remember the script is, everything in that script is strictly the psychology of something. So you get done blasting somebody with a lot of energy. Okay, and they're kind of stepped back a little bit. Maybe they're guards up a little bit. You told them you're going to fill out a financial profile on them. You can ask them personal questions. And the very first question is, do you have high speed or dial up? So it's just a settlement. Just a settling question. It's irrelevant. It doesn't matter if they have high speed or dial up. Who cares? Okay, so it's just trying to get them to relax, get them in the mode of answering your questions and responding to you. Okay. When you're going through the probe, the purpose of the probe is three main things that we need to get out of the probe. First one is going to be their pain. The second one is going to be the money. And the third one is going to be their goals. Being new at this, guys, if you have me a set, Randy a set, Robbie a set, and you don't know their pain and you don't know their goals, but you know their money, we can still get a deal. If you know their goals and you know their pain, but you don't know their money, we can't get a goal or we can't get a deal. If someone doesn't have any money, they're obviously it's not a deal. So this is the biggest thing, especially when you're new to focus on is the money and getting detailed on the money. Okay. The other thing you have to keep in mind is people lie. So you have to dig deep into the money. It is something where when you're going through the probe, you want to expand. You want to dig a little bit deeper than just how many how much credit card do you have? How much credit card debt do you have? Oh, about 15 grand. Okay. And how much credit card availability do you have left? I don't know five or six. It's okay. It's better than not getting anything at all. But that's why we have this the set or sheet spread, you know, formatted the way that we do. We want to know the type of card. We want to know the debt that's on that specific card. We want to know the interest rate because there's things that we can do for them. And we want to know the availability. You guys will get to a point to where if someone's unsure of it, you do so you do so well in your in your pain, bringing out the pain in these people that when you get to the money, they'll just give you everything. You'll get to the point where if they're not sure on this, they'll give you their 16 digits so you can call the credit card and figure out exactly where they're at. It takes time, but that all comes from doing a good job on digging out the pain. Again, Tony Robbins come Tony Robbins, his favorite saying that I've ever heard is probe for pain and magnify the hurt. Probe for pain and magnify the hurt. So Matt, what does that mean to you? The probe for pain magnify the hurt. You know, find out what their pain is and make a pretty much make a solution for it so they're not hurting. Okay. Am I correct or? When you find out their pain, constantly remind them. You're lifting up a mirror and I know some of these selling tactics guys when you're not used to it, some of it can feel uncomfortable. But if you want to be an all-star at this, people don't buy because we're going to build them a squeeze page. People don't buy because we have great coaches. People buy because they work at Walmart for eight bucks an hour, put a smile on their face, opening doors, they have no retirement. Okay. That's why people buy. So you have to magnify the hurt. So if I will role play, Clayton ring ring. Hello, Clayton, Clayton there. Hi to see. Hi, Clayton. This is Ben calling with Russell Brunson's office. How are you doing pretty well? I'm doing well. I'm doing well. Thanks for asking. I'm going to skip the intro and blast and go right into the probe and already pass some of the easy questions into your job. We're at Walmart for me. So Clinton, what is it that you're doing for work? Oh, I'm a creator at Walmart. Walmart really? How long you've been doing that? Three, three and a half years now. Okay. Okay. How many hours a week are you working? I'm putting in as many as they will give me. I'm doing about 35 or 40. Okay. Okay. Are they paying you? What's your worth, Clinton? I depends on when you say, when, how much I was worth back in the day or how much I'm worth now, I guess. Well, I mean, what are they paying you on an hourly basis? Oh, about 625 an hour. 625 an hour? Yeah. Okay. What's minimum wage these days? I think it's about 625 an hour, isn't it? And you said you're 63 years old? Yeah. So 63 years old, you've worked your whole life. You have to be somewhat of an educated man in a week and 625 an hour. That's tough to pay the bills. Sure is. How's the retirement plan? I'm not set up on one. Okay. Just a general rule of thumb and retirement, Clinton, is to have, you were supposed to have 10 times your annual salary set aside into a retirement fund just to maintain the same style of living that you're accustomed to now. So at 625 an hour, you're probably making $18,000 a year. You should have $180,000 set aside just so you can maintain the same lifestyle that you have right now. Obviously, that's not a very fun lifestyle at 625 an hour. How close to $180,000 in your retirement fund, right? I don't have any of my retirement funds. Okay, so you got ways to go and you're already at retirement age. Yeah. Okay, so chances are Clinton, unless you do something different, you're going to be opening those doors of Walmart until the day you die. That would you agree? If you want to change your financial situation, you're going to have to do something different. See that magnifying, it's the same thing with college funds. You get people on the phone and 100 kids college fund looking. You're 13 years old and you haven't done anything yet. You don't have anything set aside for your kids college fund. You smoke, smoking three bucks a day, four bucks a day, $120 a month. What's more important to you? Giving yourself that cigarette or letting your kids have an education. Okay, that's magnifying the pain or magnifying the hurt. That makes sense? Just digging deeper, digging deeper. It's also how you come across guys. There's a time and a place for it. Some of the people that we speak with, you know, they're already successful online. They are sophisticated people. It's hard to beat like Robbie was talking about the other day. People's pain is different. Right? If you're talking to a doctor who makes $200,000 a year, doesn't have any debt. It's hard to beat them up on college funds and retirement and things like that. Instead, you're beating them up on being at the hospital 120 hours a week and not seeing those kids getting raised, you know, not raising his own kids, things like that. So you have to adjust to each conversation that you're doing. But the idea through the probe is simply you have to dig out the pain. It's the biggest thing. It's why people buy. You have to get detailed information on the money. And then you have to allow the client to dream. Clinton, if I could put you in a position where you were making an extra three to $5,000 a month in and month out, what does that do for you financially? Well, I would get out of my job there and try to help my grandkids and kids out a little bit. Okay. How big of a priority is it for you to get out of your job? On a scale of one to 10, how motivated are you to get out of your job? I guess about eight or nine. Then eight or nine. Why not a 10? If you could replay, you like working at Walmart that much? No, but it's just scary. You know, for sure it is. It has to be some inclininus isn't going to be something that happens overnight. It takes time. Okay. But realistically, do you think there's people on the internet that are making three $5,000 a month? Sure. What separates you from that? You've already talked about it. The knowledge. If you knew the same things they knew and we're implementing the same things those people were doing on a daily basis, why would you get a different result? So if we can provide you with that knowledge and we can help you put your best foot forward and put you in a position where you can go in and fire your boss, what would keep you, what would hold you back from doing that? It's just constantly digging, constantly asking more questions. It's not, oh, you work at Walmart. Okay. How long you done that? Five years. Okay. Um, and then just moving on to something else. When they give you ammunition, stay on it. The script guys is an outline and you guys are all doing awesome staying on your script and getting sets and it's working. There's going to come a time when you have to veer off it a little bit. There's going to be a time where you don't just ask these, these specific questions in the pro. If someone gives you ammunition, go off it a little bit, expand on it, keep talking about it. The more detailed information that you can give us on a setter sheet, the better chance we have of making a deal. If you hand me over a setter sheet, um, you know, the page looks like this and this is our credit card and this is, this is our pain and you have two lines on pain and you don't leave out the six, you don't even fill out a six month and 12 month goal and you know, you had their name and their phone number. Kind of a bad example because we have the money and that's obviously the big thing, but you want this thing, you front and back as much information as you can, you have to listen of their, of their pain during the pros. Yeah. During the pro you have to listen. It's that, that's all you should be doing is listening 80, 20. Think that you guys should have every single conversation. Okay. Right. I want you guys to remember this. If you don't take anything else out of this training, take this in your probe every single time the client on the end of the phone should ask you if you're still there. Hello, you still there? Yeah, I'm here. I'm just taking some notes to let them know you care. It lets them know you're listening and you should be, you should be sitting down, taking notes and writing down all the ammunition that you can give because if someone tells you it might be one little hot button that is going to make that guy buy. And if I don't know it going into closing it, how am I going to sell them? If it's just that one little thing. So every, but if I have it written down and I have notes on it, it's just more ammunition I can use in the close. And so the more, the more detailed notes that you can take, the more likely your sets are going to close. And it all comes from listening when you're going to the probe. Another thing I want you guys to focus on before you turn over a set. You want to know why wouldn't the customer buy? So when you come in, you hand us a set, you can, Ryan and I had this as an example Tuesday and it's by no means Ryan's fault. They didn't bring it up to Ryan. They didn't tell me until I called him back. I went through, Ryan did an awesome job on the set. I get all the way through the close. It was a great pitch. They were just, it meaning that the customer was just excited. They liked it. They didn't have any big objections. They wanted an hour to talk about it amongst themselves and I get back on the phone with them and he had just finished a coaching program that they spent 10 grand on a month ago. Well, if I know that going into it, I can address it prior to ever spending an hour and a half on the phone with them. That's the reason why they didn't buy it. They just spent 10 grand. They haven't made any money from their coaching program. It's an objection that can be overcome at the beginning, not at the end. So the purpose of your questions and, and I think that's a good point. And obviously it's digging out the pain, finding the money and finding their goals. But we also want to know what is it that is going to keep them from buying? We want to know what their objections are going to be. If someone tells you that they've spent 10 grand on a coaching program a couple months ago, tell that before, tell us that before we call them. Let us know what their objections are going to be. And sales industry with the objections, you bring them up and brag about them, talk about them, get them out of the way before the client could ever bring them up that way. They can't use it as an excuse later. So we want to be able to eliminate all those as well. As far as goals, again, guys, it's just magnifying. It's just digging deeper. It's not rushing through things. Your set, the biggest thing with the set, I'm sure you guys have heard them say it in your other trainings, but you want to get from point A, which is when they pick up the phone, the point B, which is when you turn over the set. And you want to get there in the shortest time for impossible, meaning that you want it to be a 20 minute, 25 minute set. If you're getting into 45 minutes, you're on the phone too long. Some people are chatterboxes and you just can't get off the phone with them. You want to be at the money no later than 15 minutes. You want to be talking to people about the money. So you want to have an intro in two or three minutes, a blast in two or three minutes and be into the probe and into their money no longer than 15 minutes. If they don't have the money, get off the phone. Ben Gay and the closers will tell you, you want to spend your time with qualified people. Do I spend your time on the phone with someone that doesn't have any money? It doesn't do any of us any good. It costs the company money. So, but I get the point with A to B is, is you don't want to be talking to Sally into your intro or after you've intro'd her. You don't want to be talking to her about her dog or her daughter doing ballet or anything that's irrelevant. And it'll happen where you get into conversations and you're all over the map. So you start off with your intro and boom, you're down here talking about something you're relevant. How do you get back to this point? Jump back on the script. Ask another question. Okay. That's another question. It'll get you here and boom, they'll try taking you down here. Get back up. Ask another question. Get them back on the back on your straight line and get from point A to point B without going off the script as much as possible. So with it, with the script guys, it's, it's intended for an outline. The last thing you want to do is have these people knowing that you're reading from the script. So you want to read from the script and stick to the script, but have a conversation with these people. If you truly care about helping these people, it's going to come out in your voice and they're going to understand that. You're passionate about what you do. The sales take care of themselves. I think I go too fast. Yeah. Slow, slow down. I mean, you want to, you don't want to be on the phone with them for an hour. Right. And the main reason being is if someone, if, how, how much time do you have in your life to talk to a telemarketer that called you up out of the blue? How much time are you going to give me if I call you? I better grab your attention. Bam. I mean, right when you pick up the phone. Okay. And then when I have your attention, I better do a damn good job of keeping it until I get to here. Or else you're off the phone with me. Right. If I'm Ho-hummin and talking about your dog and your daughter's ballet and all these kinds of things, and it's a, it's a 45 minute set. Now you've just taken 45 minutes out of my day. I had no idea you were calling. I got other things I have to do. How are they going to spend another hour with me on a close? Yeah. So that leads to setting an appointment for tomorrow that doesn't answer. It's to get them now, lose them forever mentality. And the main way you do that is by getting from A to B in 20 minutes so that they can still have some time to talk to me. Grabbing their attention, keeping them excited, using a lot of energy and getting them to the point to where you've only been on the phone with them 20 minutes and you haven't used up their whole day. And you have them dreaming. You have them thinking, God, my life sucks. If I don't do something different now, what's life look like three years from now? Same. Hey, you guys, you guys are trying to say Einstein defines insanity by doing the same thing over and over again and expecting to get a different result. It's a great line. Another thing just, I mean, that kind of covers the probe for the most part, guys, but just some other just kind of pointers. This industry that we're in is saturated. There are, and what I mean by that is there's a lot of coaching programs out there, especially people. The one thing we're blessed in as a company, unless you're familiar with this industry and that you don't really understand is that typically how coaching programs are structured is, there's a sales floor in Boise. The guru lives in Dallas and the coaching departments in Sacramento. And it's all over the map. Russell, which I don't know, have you guys met? You guys been upstairs yet? He came down yesterday. I'll take you guys upstairs so you guys can meet the guys upstairs after this. But Russell's right upstairs. Our coaches are in the same apartment or, you know, same floor right here. Everything's done in-house. We don't outsource anything. So it's extremely beneficial for the client's behalf. The fulfillment here is tremendous compared to what other people are accustomed to. Okay. That being said, a lot of these people you've talked to in this industry, this script, that we didn't invent it, it works. Every other sales floor uses it. So every single one of your people that you're calling are people that buy other people's stuff and call them, get pitched by people just like you the exact same way. So if you sound like the last guy that sold them a $10,000 coaching package and you're saying the same things that guy said, and they didn't make any money when they use that coaching program, what are the chances they're going to buy from you? You have to do something different. You have to grab people's attention. You have to have unique selling propositions. And the way you do that is by showing that you care instead of that you're just there for the sale. So it takes time, yes. It's not something where you can't look at this as a, gotta hit zero this week. Man, I've hit zero two weeks in a row. This sucks. You have to look, stick it out six months and see where you're at in six months from now. If you haven't made 30 grand in six months, you're probably right. It's probably not for you. But just because you have two or three weeks where you don't get a sale, it's, it's a continuing learning process. Randy's, Randy and I both have been doing it for three years. And the whole point is, is you need to be working at getting better every single day and you guys are blessed to work at a company to where this sales floor is going to be full here pretty quick. We're going to have 120 guys in here. So each of us will have, you know, we'll, we're all, all going to have positions, whether it's your, your setting or you're a team leader and you have 10 guys underneath you that are setting up. There, there's position, there's opportunity to grow here. And if you continue to get better and continue to understand the process and you can take one of these positions like Randy has where you have every guy on the floor setting up or a team of 10, that, that's the end result. It takes time to get there. It's taken Randy three years, but three years down the road from now, you're making 150 grand a year. So that's, that's the big perk of, of what we do here. It's an awesome opportunity as far as the company is concerned within this industry. And just because of the resources that we have, the things that they do, the money that they spend on their fulfillment on generating leads. The biggest kicker with this industry is leads. It's the hardest part. It's why people, sales floors go out of business all the time. It's because they run out of leads. We have our ups and downs. That happens, but they'll be awesome guys and Garrett Russell and Tom. I don't know if you guys have met Tom, Tom kind of oversees the lead generation. He's the vice president of marketing here. They, they bust their tails to keep leads in our hands and to make sure, you know, if we're not making money, they're not making money and they understand that. It's not, it's not a greed built company. So 10 commandments of sales success. And then we'll, we'll cut out of here. And this comes out of the sales Bible. Some of it, keep in mind relates to face to face sales. You can always implement it into what we do over the phone. Number one thing is to think. When he talks about thinking, he's simply saying that the sale is in your head. We talked about attitude on Monday. It's having the psyche every time you sit down and dial your phone guys. Honestly, if you're having a bad day, if you've sent over three sets, like Ryan, I know we got a little frustrating. You send over three good sets and none of them go. Something happens to them. Credit cards are expired or their wife sucks or, you know, whatever the case may be and they just don't go and it pisses you off. Go leave for an hour. That's the thing you want to do is dial and burn your leads when you're upset or you're not in the right mind frame. You have to have the right mindset every time you dial on the phone. We're leave, take a break, get out for a half an hour. No one cares. As long as you, you know, that's your reason for leaving. You come back and you work hard with the right mindset. Number two is to believe in what you're selling. I don't know how else to say it other than I've worked on other sales floors and the fulfillment here is second to none. And I've been in the industry for a while. The people that we're working with, if they fall through, they put in the time and they do the effort, they make money with it. It's just a matter of getting people to actually get off the couch. But you have to, 100% without a doubt, believe in the product you're selling or you're never going to get a sell. Number three is to engage. Develop rapport and personal engagement or don't start selling, or don't start the selling conversation. It comes back to that chart I was showing you. A to B, you have to engage people. You have to get their interest. You have to excite them right out the gates. If you make someone laugh, that engages them. Number four is discover. What he means by discover is that people buy for their reasons, not yours. Find out what their reasons are for buying. It comes down to paying. Painting goals. Number five is ask. Ask the wrong questions and you're going to get the wrong answers. The other side of that obviously is ask the right questions. Six is observe. Your ability to observe must be as powerful as your ability to sell and to listen. I think in our industry, the observing is more so just listening to the people. Number seven is dare. Have the balls to ask the tough questions. The future is in the hands of the asker, the person that will ask the questions. If you don't ask, the answer is always no. If you have someone you think you're rubbing wrong with, call them out on it. If someone's just yes, yes, yes and yes, call them out. I do it all the time, especially if I'm on pitch, but if I'm in a set and I'm into the set more than three minutes and they still have CNN turned up to freaking 50 on their volume, tell them to turn off the TV or get off the phone with them, the phone with them. It's more important than watching your news for 30 minutes or changing your life. So how do the balls to ask the tough questions? AID is earned. Obviously we're all here to make a paycheck. At the end of the day that's what it comes down to. Again we talked about a lot of this yesterday so I don't want to rehash on it but the way he words it is sell for the relationship and not the commission. Sell for the fact that you're going to change someone's life and give someone a better opportunity rather than the commission and the commission will take care of themselves. Number nine is own. You have to know whose fault it is when the sell isn't made. And your guys' behalf you have to know whose fault it was if you didn't get a set over. If you kick a set over and I don't get the sell it's absolutely my fault you did your job go on. You have to have that mindset that you're doing your job right. But if you get someone on the phone that you think is good and you couldn't get them over as a set think about it. What's that one question I could have asked that maybe would have turned them into a set? So own up to it and become. Act as if is how we say act as if you're making $150,000 a year as a business consultant for Russell Freikin Brunson. People love this guy. So when you're talking to people and they're telling you thank you your responses you're welcome. It's not thank you. It's thank you for your time. It is because none of us are dicks. It is difficult. But it's positioning yourself and posturing yourself and your director is hey my time is more valuable than yours. And if you want help I can help you. And it's all in how you come across to these people. So I'm becoming says you don't get great at selling in a day you get great at selling day by day. And that's why I put those books up. Consistently study guys consistently read consistently work at it. Now it's hard we're here most of the day as it is but for guys that don't go out on lunch. Any questions I mean that's we're all in this together. You know Randy myself Cody we don't make money unless you guys are handed over a lot of sets. So a good goal for you guys that read out the gates is one one good set every single day. You should have a set every day. Nothing to replace all this training guys that you're going to get because that's one thing about this company every Monday and Wednesday. You're going to get more training than you know what to do with and nothing replaces the 70 dials three hours of phone time. Hundred dials a day. Nothing replaces it. A guy I worked with on my last floor the absolute worst salesman in the world. Terrible. He just but the one thing he did is the guy would have like 400 dials in a day. Any old lead any lead anybody wanted to give him that guy sat there. He didn't come in. He didn't talk to anybody. He just put his head down and he dialed and read the script verbatim and the guy had like a seven thousand dollar average and he was terrible at it. But it was just because he would dial 400 times a day. So it's a numbers game sales more money throw against law so instead. Any questions on it. Could you share some other problem questions that you've had a better other than what are on. Yeah I put some down in here and I just put them in regards to if I'm talking to someone about my credit card debt or their job for instance. Okay. How long have you been working at your job. How long have you been doing that line of work. How many hours a week do you work. They pay you what you worth. What's your worth. What type of benefits do they provide. What type of time off do you have with your family. Just expanding I guess on credit card debt. How much of that 15 thousand dollars of credit card that's making you money. How much of that's positive. How long have you carried. How long have you carried that amount of debt. What is your minimum payment. If you didn't have that debt. What could you do with the money that is now allocated to those payments every month. 15 thousand dollars in credit card debt and their payment their minimum payment. 10 10 grand 300 bucks minimum probably closer to five. Five probably. It's typically 2% that's what we use on our end because that's the low side of it. So when we're trying to justify the payments of people on the 10 thousand dollar investments only 200 bucks a month chances are it's probably 250 to 3 depending on your credit card and your interest and things like that. But if you spend more time. Especially when you find someone when you find that good voice and they have 15 thousand dollars in credit card debt and they're making $8 an hour. And they still have 15 thousand available. That should be a deal every single time. And it's just how you ask these questions and if you expand on versus if you just say OK 15 grand and debt 15 available. OK. High speed or dialogue. Moving on to something completely different. Stay on it. Continue to talk about it. Ask the right questions. You don't want to be talking to them about something completely relevant but spend your time. If you can spend 45 minutes on the phone talking about $15 thousand dollars in credit card debt by all means do it. But the point is is to ask the right questions in regards to the script. How long is it going to take you to pay off that 15 thousand dollars in debt doing what you're doing right now. So in five years from now you might have that credit card that paid off my bed as it takes you 20. You think you need to get new smoke. You eat out. What's more important to you. Your kids college fund or the double cheeseburger with a cigarette. Sometimes those double cheeseburgers. You know it's guys that it comes down to breaking people down and building a backup. You'll get it to where you have people crying on the phone. That's when you've done your job. If you're just lifting up a mirror. You don't do it in a demeaning manner. And there's some guys that do. It's not something that we do as a company. And if it is something you're doing you want job. They don't they don't stand for it. It's not how we operate. That being said there's a right way to ask these questions. There's nothing offensive with me saying how long is it going to take you to pay off 15 grand in credit card. That's four years at Boise State. That's a college education for your kid. Get out of that debt. Do something different. I mean any around any specific question are you wondering to expand on those questions. No that's what I needed. Some of those. And I think I was proven too much in the introduction. That's what I'm gathering from your conversation. I'm spending too much time in while establishing rapport. Asking the probing questions without blasting yet. So the excitement's not there for them to give you the information that you need. That might be putting them off a little bit. Those are pretty much the objections you're going to have to overcome. And after you've said it enough times and heard the same objection over and over and over again it becomes pretty easy. I spend five minutes on the introduction. One thing I try to stay away from on the introduction is how's the weather. It's just talk about the people love talking about themselves. My favorite thing to do is talk about myself. My kids, my wife, my life. Everybody's that way. They want to talk about themselves. So you do do a little bit of probing in the introduction. So, but, yeah, I guess the intro I would spend, I spent two to five minutes on it. Big question I like to ask in the intro is what their niche is, because it gets them talking about themselves. And you have to remember, every single one of these people had bought something at some point in time, relating to starting an internet business. So if you talk to them about their niche, that gets them talking about themselves, and what they're looking to do, and gives you some ammo. The blast should be, like Randy probably told you, two to three minutes of as much energy as you have. Like I said, I went on tangent a little bit, but I'll dial through my leads until I get that right voice. And then, and when I get through a good introduction, and when I'm about to blast someone, I'm out of my chair. And I'm standing up, my voice is stronger, and I have a lot more energy, and I'm going to blast them as hard as I can for two minutes. And then you have high speed or dial up. Their credit's excellent. I'm going to give them the benefit of the doubt and try to do my job so that I can get the information when I need to get it. Right.
